stem: You are the GP reviewing a 28-year-old IT worker who requests assessment for ADHD after reading about it online. He describes lifelong difficulty completing tasks, frequent job changes, chronic disorganisation, losing items and being easily distracted. School reports from primary school describe him as bright but unable to focus and impulsive. He has no current substance use, no past psychosis, and his mood is stable. He sleeps adequately. He has not previously been on any psychiatric medication. He is now in a stable relationship.

data_table.Examination: Alert, restless, normal mental state otherwise, no thought disorder, no psychotic symptoms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence a trial of dexamphetamine 5 mg twice daily for 4 weeks and review symptom response, with referral to specialist ADHD service only if the medication trial does not show clear benefit
options.rationale: Stimulant prescription requires specialist diagnosis and an authority script; trial first is not appropriate in primary care. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Order brain MRI, sleep study, urine drug screen, and thyroid function to exclude organic mimics of inattention before any referral to mental health services is initiated by primary care
options.rationale: Routine neuroimaging and sleep study are not standard ADHD work-up; selected screening tests may be appropriate but not all of these. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Reassure the patient that adult-onset ADHD does not exist and arrange counselling for procrastination, with cognitive behavioural therapy as the primary intervention for organisational difficulty
options.rationale: ADHD persists into adulthood in 50 to 70% of childhood cases; refusing assessment is not appropriate when symptoms are present since childhood. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist or paediatrician credentialed in adult ADHD assessment to confirm diagnosis using DSM-5 criteria, collateral history, and validated rating scales before any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Formal diagnosis by an appropriately credentialed specialist using DSM-5 criteria and collateral history is required before stimulant prescription. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Prescribe atomoxetine 40 mg daily for 4 weeks as a non-stimulant trial, since it requires no authority script in adults and is safe to initiate in general practice without specialist input
options.rationale: Atomoxetine in adults still requires specialist diagnosis; first prescription is by a specialist with GP shared care thereafter. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 criteria: five or more symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity, several present before age 12, present in 2 or more settings, causing functional impairment, and not better explained by another condition. Assessment uses clinical interview with validated tools (DIVA-5, ASRS) and collateral history. Comorbidities are common - anxiety, depression, substance use, sleep disorders, ASD - and require parallel management. Stimulants (methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine, dexamfetamine) are first-line treatment; non-stimulant atomoxetine or guanfacine are alternatives. Specialist credentialling rules vary by state but all currently require psychiatrist or paediatrician initiation in Australia.
key_points: DSM-5 needs childhood onset and 2+ settings.
Use validated rating scales and collateral history.
Screen for common comorbidities.
Stimulants are first-line; non-stimulants second.
Specialist diagnosis required for first prescription.
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine has lower abuse potential than immediate-release dexamphetamine because it requires red cell hydrolysis to produce active drug, giving a smoother onset and longer duration.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem:
You are the consultation liaison psychiatry registrar called to a 67-year-old man on the medical ward 5 days after admission for community-acquired pneumonia, which has resolved. Nursing staff report he has stopped eating and drinking and has not spoken for 48 hours. He had a major depressive episode 2 years ago that responded to sertraline, which his wife stopped during this admission because of new medications.

On examination he is awake but mute, holds his left arm in an awkward raised posture for over a minute when placed, and resists passive movement with stiff opposition.

data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, eye contact reduced, waxy flexibility, mitgehen, no rigidity outside posturing, no tremor, no fever, no autonomic instability
data_table.Investigations: FBE normal, UEC Na 138 K 4.2 creatinine 78, CK 220, TSH 1.8, CRP 12, CT brain unremarkable, urine MCS no growth
data_table.Medications: Withheld sertraline; received ceftriaxone, azithromycin during admission, now ceased

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge with reassessment for clinical response at 5 to 10 minutes after the dose has been given
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia and is the standard first step before considering ECT. (Source: RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders, BMJ Best Practice Catatonia)

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 50 mg now to address agitation and restore behavioural control quickly, with nasogastric feed if intake remains poor overnight
options.rationale: Antipsychotics, especially typicals, can precipitate or worsen catatonia and provoke neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are contraindicated. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Catatonia)

options.text: Arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy for first treatment within 24 hours without a lorazepam challenge given the severe presentation and the patient's prior depressive history
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge is the standard initial test and treatment; ECT is reserved for lorazepam non-response or malignant catatonia. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Catatonia)

options.text: Restart sertraline 50 mg orally daily and observe for 1 week, supporting nutrition with nasogastric feeding, and review progress at the next ward round before considering escalation
options.rationale: SSRI alone takes too long and does not treat acute catatonia; lorazepam is the immediate intervention. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Catatonia)

options.text: Commence intravenous dantrolene and bromocriptine combination for presumed neuroleptic malignant syndrome based on rigidity, mutism and recent hospitalisation with antibiotic exposure
options.rationale: NMS requires fever, marked autonomic instability and very high CK; this patient lacks these features and there has been no antipsychotic exposure. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of psychomotor disturbance characterised by features such as mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, mitgehen, negativism, echolalia and stereotypies. It complicates mood disorders, schizophrenia and general medical conditions. Diagnosis is supported by the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale; 2 or more of 14 signs supports the diagnosis. The lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV) gives both diagnostic confirmation and therapeutic benefit in around 70 to 80% within hours. Maintenance is regular lorazepam 6 to 24 mg daily titrated to response. ECT is the most effective treatment and is preferred for malignant catatonia (fever, autonomic instability, rigidity) which approaches NMS in mortality. Avoid antipsychotics.
key_points: Diagnose using Bush-Francis criteria (2 of 14 signs).
Lorazepam challenge: diagnostic and therapeutic.
Avoid antipsychotics - may precipitate NMS.
ECT for lorazepam non-response or malignant catatonia.
Identify underlying cause: mood, psychotic, or medical.
explanation.pearls: Mitgehen is the anglepoise-lamp sign where the patient's arm moves with even the lightest touch despite instructions to resist - one of the most specific catatonic signs.

stem:
You are the GP reviewing a 34-year-old female paramedic 4 months after a fatal multi-casualty incident on a highway. She describes intrusive flashbacks several times daily, nightmares, avoidance of driving past the site, hypervigilance at work, irritability and low mood. She is no longer enjoying activities she once loved. There is no substance misuse. She had a brief depressive episode in her 20s.

She is functioning at work with difficulty and has supportive family. She is not suicidal and has no current self-harm. She has not previously had psychotherapy.

data_table.Examination: Mood low, affect reactive, hypervigilant during interview, no thought disorder, no psychotic symptoms, oriented

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily as first-line pharmacotherapy and provide psychoeducation, with referral for psychological therapy only if symptoms have not responded after 12 weeks of medication
options.rationale: Trauma-focused psychological therapy is first-line for PTSD; pharmacotherapy is second-line or used in parallel when psychotherapy is unavailable. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing, either delivered by a trained therapist over 8 to 12 sessions as first-line management
options.rationale: TF-CBT or EMDR are first-line for PTSD with strongest evidence; both are equally effective. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe benzodiazepine clonazepam 0.5 mg twice daily for anxiety and sleep disturbance, combined with stress management techniques and lifestyle advice in primary care without further referral
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen PTSD outcomes by impairing fear extinction learning and risk dependence; they are contraindicated. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Provide single-session psychological debriefing focused on the index incident, with review at 4 weeks to determine whether additional structured therapy will be needed for any persisting symptoms
options.rationale: Single-session debriefing does not prevent PTSD and may worsen outcomes; structured trauma-focused therapy is required. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission for stabilisation and intensive group therapy given the severity of intrusive symptoms and the operational role she holds in emergency services
options.rationale: PTSD without psychosis, suicidality or inability to function is managed in the community; admission is not indicated. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: PTSD is diagnosed when characteristic symptoms (intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, arousal) follow exposure to a traumatic event and persist beyond 1 month with functional impairment. First-line treatment is trauma-focused psychological therapy delivered by a trained clinician: trauma-focused CBT and EMDR both have strong evidence. Pharmacotherapy with sertraline, paroxetine or venlafaxine is second-line or adjunct when therapy is unavailable, declined, or insufficient. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated because they impair fear extinction. Prazosin is added for trauma nightmares. Antipsychotic augmentation is for severe treatment-resistant cases. Single-session debriefing is no longer recommended.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR are first-line.
Sertraline, paroxetine or venlafaxine if pharmacotherapy needed.
Avoid benzodiazepines.
Add prazosin for trauma-related nightmares.
Single-session debriefing does not prevent PTSD.
explanation.pearls: Prazosin reduces noradrenergic activity through alpha-1 blockade and is started at 1 mg at night titrated to 5 to 15 mg; first-dose hypotension is the main precaution especially in older adults.

stem: You are the ED registrar on the late shift when security calls for help. A 30-year-old man with known schizophrenia is shouting, pacing, threatening staff and refusing to engage. He has been brought in by police under emergency apprehension for self-neglect. He has previously responded to oral olanzapine in similar presentations. Verbal de-escalation by senior nursing staff over 20 minutes has not reduced agitation, and the team is now concerned for the safety of other patients and staff in the department. He has no known allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Not yet measurable, observed alert with regular respirations, no pallor, no diaphoresis
data_table.Examination: Pacing, shouting, raised arms, persecutory and grandiose content, refusing assessment
data_table.Medications: Documented past response to oral olanzapine; on community paliperidone depot, last dose 5 weeks ago (overdue)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Restrain physically and administer intramuscular zuclopenthixol acetate 100 mg for sustained tranquillisation, then re-dose after 24 hours if behavioural disturbance has not fully settled
options.rationale: Long-acting zuclopenthixol acetate is not appropriate for acute behavioural disturbance because its 48 to 72 hour effect cannot be titrated. (Source: SHPA Acute Behavioural Disturbance Statement)

options.text: Administer intramuscular droperidol 10 mg plus midazolam 5 mg without offering oral therapy, given the level of disturbance and the risk to other patients in the department
options.rationale: Oral medication should be offered first when feasible; combination IM droperidol plus midazolam carries respiratory depression risk and is reserved for failure of monotherapy. (Source: SHPA Acute Behavioural Disturbance Statement)

options.text: Administer intravenous ketamine 200 mg as procedural sedation by the consultant, with rapid sequence intubation prepared because of the level of agitation and refusal to engage with staff
options.rationale: Ketamine sedation is reserved for severe refractory cases requiring physical restraint with anaesthetic backup; not first-line. (Source: SHPA Acute Behavioural Disturbance Statement)

options.text: Administer intramuscular haloperidol 10 mg with promethazine 50 mg as a single combination injection, then commence regular oral antipsychotic once cooperative for the remainder of the admission
options.rationale: High-dose haloperidol with promethazine carries QT prolongation and over-sedation risk; modern Australian practice favours droperidol or olanzapine first. (Source: SHPA Acute Behavioural Disturbance Statement)

options.text: Offer oral olanzapine wafer 10 mg as the first pharmacological step alongside continued de-escalation, accepting that intramuscular medication is offered only if oral is refused
options.rationale: Oral medication is preferred over IM where the patient can accept it; olanzapine wafer is rapid-onset and matches his past response. (Source: SHPA Acute Behavioural Disturbance Statement)

explanation.summary: Acute behavioural disturbance management follows a stepped approach: verbal de-escalation first, then offered oral medication, then intramuscular monotherapy, then IM combination if needed. Olanzapine wafer 5 to 10 mg is a fast-acting oral option that matches the patient's previous response. IM options are droperidol 10 mg or olanzapine 10 mg; combination IM droperidol plus midazolam is reserved for severe disturbance with respiratory monitoring. Avoid IM olanzapine within 1 hour of IM benzodiazepine because of respiratory depression. Continuous monitoring of airway, breathing and circulation is mandatory after any IM sedation. Document Mental Health Act status and review medication regularly.
key_points: Verbal de-escalation always first.
Offer oral medication before IM where possible.
Droperidol or olanzapine IM are monotherapy options.
Avoid IM olanzapine with IM benzodiazepine within 1 hour.
Continuous airway, breathing and circulation monitoring after IM.
explanation.pearls: Droperidol carries a black box QT warning but at typical sedation doses (5 to 10 mg IM) the absolute torsades risk is very low and ECG screening is reserved for risk factors or repeated dosing.

subtopic_detail: Suspected giant cell arteritis - initiation of treatment
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 74-year-old woman with 3 days of severe right-sided temporal headache, jaw claudication after a few minutes of chewing, and a brief episode of blurred vision in her right eye that resolved after 30 minutes this morning. She has been generally fatigued for 6 weeks. Past history includes hypertension and osteoporosis on alendronate. Examination reveals tenderness over the right temporal artery, which is non-pulsatile and feels thickened. Visual acuity is currently 6/6 bilaterally and fundoscopy is normal.

data_table.Examination: Right temporal artery tender, thickened, pulseless; no proximal limb weakness, no scalp necrosis, neuro exam normal, fundoscopy normal
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116, platelets 444, WCC 9.8, ESR 92, CRP 78, ALP normal, creatinine 74
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily; alendronate 70 mg weekly; calcium 600 mg, vitamin D 1000 IU daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily immediately, refer urgently for temporal artery biopsy within 1 week, and arrange ophthalmology review the same day for visual symptom assessment
options.rationale: GCA with amaurosis fugax requires immediate high-dose oral or IV steroid to prevent permanent vision loss; biopsy follows within 1 to 2 weeks. (Source: eTG Giant Cell Arteritis)

options.text: Arrange temporal artery biopsy first to confirm the diagnosis histologically before commencing any corticosteroid therapy that might confound interpretation of the histological vessel inflammation
options.rationale: Delaying steroids risks irreversible visual loss; treat first, biopsy after - histology remains diagnostic for up to 2 weeks. (Source: eTG Giant Cell Arteritis)

options.text: Prescribe oral aspirin 100 mg daily, refer for urgent vascular ultrasound of the temporal artery, and review the result before deciding on initiation of corticosteroid therapy at a later date
options.rationale: Aspirin alone does not treat the underlying vasculitis and waiting for imaging delays sight-saving steroid. (Source: eTG Giant Cell Arteritis)

options.text: Prescribe paracetamol regularly for headache, arrange GP review in 1 week with repeat inflammatory markers, and refer to general medicine if the clinical features have not settled by then
options.rationale: Suspected GCA with amaurosis fugax is a sight-threatening emergency; analgesia alone is inadequate. (Source: eTG Giant Cell Arteritis)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 15 mg daily and refer to rheumatology outpatients for assessment within 4 weeks, providing patient information about polymyalgia rheumatica and steroid management plan
options.rationale: 15 mg is a polymyalgia rheumatica dose; GCA needs 40 to 60 mg daily (or 1 g pulsed methylprednisolone with visual loss) to suppress vasculitis. (Source: eTG Giant Cell Arteritis)

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) presents in patients over 50 with new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, transient or permanent visual loss, and constitutional symptoms. ESR and CRP are typically elevated. Treatment must begin immediately on clinical suspicion to prevent irreversible vision loss: oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily (1 mg/kg) without visual symptoms; IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days when visual loss is present. Temporal artery biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard and remains positive for up to 2 weeks after starting steroid. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing in relapse or intolerance. Bone protection and PPI are added with high-dose steroid.
key_points: Treat on clinical suspicion - do not wait for biopsy.
Visual loss requires IV pulse methylprednisolone.
ESR and CRP usually elevated; biopsy is gold standard.
Biopsy yield preserved for up to 2 weeks on steroid.
Bone protection and PPI with high-dose steroid.
explanation.pearls: The halo sign on temporal artery ultrasound (hypoechoic ring around the vessel wall) has high specificity for GCA and is a non-invasive alternative for diagnostic confirmation when biopsy is delayed.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice Giant Cell Arteritis

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 56-year-old man with a 2-day history of a painful, hot, expanding erythematous area over his left shin since gardening barefoot last week. There is no systemic upset, no fever, and no diabetes. He has had no previous cellulitis. He has no penicillin allergy. The erythema is sharply demarcated, warm and tender, with no fluctuance, no necrotic centre, and no skin breakdown beyond a small healed abrasion. He is able to weight-bear and there is no joint involvement. He is otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Sharply demarcated erythema 12 cm diameter over left anterior shin, warm and tender, no fluctuance, no lymphangitis, small healed abrasion centrally, inguinal nodes not enlarged
data_table.Investigations: Not routinely required for uncomplicated cellulitis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic?

options.text: Oral cephalexin 500 mg orally four times daily for 5 days, with arm or leg elevation, demarcating the leading edge in pen, and review at 48 hours to ensure clinical improvement
options.rationale: Cephalexin is first-line for non-severe cellulitis in Australia, covering Streptococcus pyogenes and methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. (Source: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Oral amoxicillin clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days as broad spectrum cover including Gram negatives, with anti-inflammatory cover using oral ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is broader than required for typical limb cellulitis and not first-line; reserved for animal or human bite or polymicrobial wounds. (Source: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days to cover community-associated MRSA, accepting that doxycycline gives suboptimal Streptococcus cover compared to a beta lactam antibiotic
options.rationale: Doxycycline is added when MRSA is suspected, not used as monotherapy because of inadequate streptococcal cover. (Source: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g six hourly via outpatient parenteral antibiotic service for 5 days, with daily nurse review and observation for failure of erythema demarcation to improve
options.rationale: IV therapy is reserved for severe cellulitis, sepsis or oral failure; non-severe cellulitis is managed with oral cephalexin or dicloxacillin. (Source: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days as targeted streptococcal cover, with topical mupirocin to the abrasion site to address the likely entry portal
options.rationale: Penicillin alone misses Staphylococcus aureus which can also cause non-purulent cellulitis; cephalexin or dicloxacillin is preferred. (Source: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

explanation.summary: Cellulitis is a dermal and subcutaneous infection commonly caused by Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus. Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess) is treated with oral cephalexin or dicloxacillin for 5 days in non-severe disease. Severe cellulitis with systemic features, rapid spread, immunocompromise, or facial location warrants IV flucloxacillin. MRSA cover (doxycycline or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is added for known MRSA colonisation, recent residential aged care, recent IV drug use or recurrent infections. Demarcate the leading edge to monitor response and elevate the limb. Failure to improve at 48 hours warrants reassessment for abscess, necrotising fasciitis, or unusual pathogen.
key_points: Cephalexin 500 mg QID for 5 days is first-line.
IV flucloxacillin for severe disease or sepsis.
Add MRSA cover for known risk factors.
Demarcate margin and elevate the limb.
Reassess at 48 hours if no improvement.
explanation.pearls: Bilateral lower limb erythema is rarely cellulitis - consider venous stasis dermatitis, lipodermatosclerosis or contact dermatitis since cellulitis is almost always unilateral.

sources.label: eTG Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

subtopic_detail: Hyperthyroidism in pregnancy - first trimester management
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 29-year-old G2P1 at 9 weeks gestation referred by the GP for thyroid abnormalities found on routine bloods. She has tremor, palpitations, heat intolerance and a 3 kg weight loss despite a good appetite. There is no family history of thyroid disease. She has no eye signs. She is not currently taking any medication.

Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. The midwife notes a goitre is palpable. The infant's heart rate is 158 on Doppler. There is no neck pain or recent viral illness.

data_table.Examination: Fine tremor, smooth diffuse goitre 2x normal size, no nodules palpable, no exophthalmos, no lid lag, no pretibial myxoedema, hyperreflexia
data_table.Investigations: TSH less than 0.01, free T4 38, free T3 12.4, TSH receptor antibodies positive, beta-hCG 90000, viability scan 9+2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence carbimazole 15 mg daily and titrate based on monthly free T4, refer to endocrinology and high-risk obstetrics, and aim for free T4 in the upper third of the non-pregnant reference range
options.rationale: Carbimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia); switch to propylthiouracil until end of first trimester. (Source: RANZCOG Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence propylthiouracil 100 mg three times daily, refer to endocrinology and high-risk obstetrics, switch to carbimazole at start of second trimester, and monitor free T4 monthly
options.rationale: Propylthiouracil is preferred in the first trimester because carbimazole has a teratogenic window then; switch to carbimazole after 12 to 14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Reassure as transient gestational hyperthyroidism, recheck thyroid function at 16 weeks, and reserve antithyroid drug therapy for persistent biochemical abnormality at the second trimester review
options.rationale: Symptomatic Graves disease with positive TSH receptor antibodies is not transient gestational thyrotoxicosis; treatment is required. (Source: RANZCOG Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange radioactive iodine ablation now to permanently resolve the hyperthyroid state and prevent fetal exposure to antithyroid drugs through the placenta over the remainder of the pregnancy
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy as it crosses the placenta and ablates the fetal thyroid. (Source: RANZCOG Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence propranolol 20 mg three times daily as monotherapy for symptom control, defer definitive antithyroid drug therapy until after delivery to avoid placental transfer to the developing fetus
options.rationale: Beta blockers control symptoms but do not treat the underlying hyperthyroidism, which carries fetal and maternal risk if untreated. (Source: RANZCOG Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Untreated Graves disease in pregnancy increases miscarriage, preterm birth, pre-eclampsia, congenital malformations and thyroid storm. Distinguish from transient gestational thyrotoxicosis, which is beta-hCG mediated, peaks at 10 to 12 weeks, occurs without TRAb and resolves by 20 weeks. First-line therapy in Graves in pregnancy is propylthiouracil 50 to 150 mg TDS in the first trimester (carbimazole linked to embryopathy in weeks 6 to 10), switching to carbimazole 5 to 15 mg daily from the second trimester (PTU has higher hepatotoxicity risk). Aim for free T4 at the upper limit of normal. Monitor TRAb in late pregnancy as high levels predict neonatal hyperthyroidism. Beta-blockers add symptom control.
key_points: PTU first trimester, switch to carbimazole second trimester.
Aim for free T4 at upper limit of normal.
Radioactive iodine absolutely contraindicated.
Distinguish Graves from transient gestational thyrotoxicosis.
Monitor TRAb in third trimester for neonatal risk.
explanation.pearls: TSH receptor antibody titre over 3 times the upper limit of normal in the third trimester predicts neonatal Graves disease and warrants paediatric endocrinology handover before delivery.

sources.label: ATA Guidelines on Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy

stem: You are the geriatric medicine registrar reviewing a 73-year-old man in the rehab ward with a 12-month progressive cognitive decline characterised by fluctuating attention, recurrent vivid visual hallucinations of small animals, and bradykinesia with a shuffling gait. His wife reports he acts out his dreams at night. Cognition fluctuates from near-normal to disoriented over hours. He has been agitated overnight on the ward and the after-hours doctor is considering a sedating antipsychotic to manage the night-time disturbance for the rest of the admission.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 78, BP 124/72 lying, BP 102/64 standing, RR 16, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Bradykinesia, mild cogwheel rigidity bilaterally, MMSE 22/30 with marked fluctuation across the assessment, no focal neurology, no seizure activity
data_table.Investigations: Hb 132, UEC normal, TSH 1.9, B12 320, glucose 5.4, CT brain age-related atrophy, MRI excluded vascular dementia, DaTscan reduced striatal uptake

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of his nocturnal behavioural disturbance?

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg at night for behavioural disturbance, with reassessment in 1 week to assess for sedation, parkinsonism worsening, or any other adverse effects on motor function
options.rationale: Antipsychotics, including atypicals such as olanzapine, cause severe sensitivity reactions in Lewy body dementia and are generally avoided. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

options.text: Stop the donepezil because cholinesterase inhibitors may aggravate behavioural disturbance, and substitute with quetiapine 25 mg at night for sedation and management of visual hallucinations
options.rationale: Donepezil should continue - it improves cognition and reduces hallucinations in DLB; stopping it would worsen the clinical picture. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

options.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg at night for hallucinations and behavioural disturbance, accepting the small risk of extrapyramidal side effects in older patients with cognitive impairment
options.rationale: Typical antipsychotics like haloperidol are particularly dangerous in DLB, causing severe parkinsonism, cognitive worsening and increased mortality. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

options.text: Optimise non-pharmacological interventions, exclude delirium triggers (infection, constipation, urinary retention, pain), continue cholinesterase inhibitor, and consider low-dose melatonin for sleep regulation
options.rationale: Non-pharmacological approaches with delirium screen and cholinesterase inhibitor optimisation are first-line; antipsychotics carry up to 50% severe sensitivity reaction rate. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

options.text: Commence levodopa-carbidopa 100/25 mg three times daily to address the parkinsonism, accepting that this may worsen hallucinations but provides motor benefit for the patient's gait disturbance
options.rationale: Levodopa is reserved for disabling motor symptoms and used cautiously because it worsens hallucinations and is not first-line in DLB. (Source: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

explanation.summary: Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) presents with the core triad of fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism. Supportive features include REM sleep behaviour disorder, severe neuroleptic sensitivity, and reduced striatal dopamine transporter uptake on DaTscan. Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) improve cognition and reduce hallucinations and are first-line. Antipsychotics provoke severe sensitivity reactions (parkinsonism, autonomic instability, cognitive worsening, NMS-like crisis, mortality) in up to half of patients; even quetiapine carries risk. Manage behavioural disturbance with delirium screen, environmental measures, sleep hygiene, melatonin and cholinesterase optimisation first. When antipsychotic is unavoidable, very low-dose quetiapine is the least bad option.
key_points: Core DLB: fluctuation, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism.
REM sleep behaviour disorder is supportive.
Cholinesterase inhibitor first-line for cognition and hallucinations.
Avoid antipsychotics - severe sensitivity in up to 50%.
Screen for delirium and reversible triggers.
explanation.pearls: Reduced striatal uptake on dopamine transporter (DaT) imaging differentiates DLB from Alzheimer disease with high specificity and supports DLB diagnosis when clinical features are equivocal.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice Dementia with Lewy Bodies

stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man for outpatient follow-up after a confirmed right lower limb deep vein thrombosis 3 months ago. The DVT followed elective right total knee replacement and was diagnosed at day 5 postoperatively. He is on apixaban 5 mg twice daily after a loading dose at presentation. He is otherwise well, has had no symptoms since the event, has no personal or family history of VTE, and is not on hormones. He is back to his usual activity level and his postoperative recovery is otherwise excellent.

data_table.Examination: Knee well healed, no calf swelling, no tenderness, no chest signs, no new neurology
data_table.Investigations: FBE normal, UEC creatinine 78, LFTs normal, INR baseline 1.0, repeat duplex ultrasound shows residual non-occlusive thrombus, no recurrence
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg BD; paracetamol PRN for occasional knee pain

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing anticoagulation plan?

options.text: Stop apixaban and commence aspirin 100 mg daily indefinitely for secondary prevention against recurrent venous thromboembolism, with review by haematology before any future major surgery
options.rationale: Aspirin gives only modest VTE recurrence reduction; routine prescription is not standard after provoked DVT once anticoagulation is complete. (Source: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE)
source_refs: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

options.text: Continue apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely because residual thrombus on ultrasound indicates ongoing prothrombotic state and conveys a high risk of recurrence without long-term anticoagulation
options.rationale: Residual venous thrombus alone does not mandate indefinite anticoagulation in surgically provoked DVT; the provoking factor has resolved. (Source: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE)
source_refs: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

options.text: Reduce apixaban to 2.5 mg twice daily and continue indefinitely for extended secondary prevention, with annual review of bleeding risk and renal function to maintain ongoing anticoagulation safety
options.rationale: Extended low-dose secondary prevention is for unprovoked VTE with acceptable bleeding risk, not surgically provoked events with no recurrent risk factor. (Source: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE)
source_refs: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

options.text: Switch from apixaban to warfarin with INR target 2 to 3 for ongoing therapy and arrange thrombophilia screening including factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation and lupus anticoagulant
options.rationale: Thrombophilia screening is not indicated for provoked VTE because it does not change duration of anticoagulation in this scenario. (Source: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE)
source_refs: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

options.text: Stop apixaban now at the standard 3 months and provide written advice on VTE recurrence symptoms with safety net advice for return if any new chest, leg or breathlessness symptoms develop
options.rationale: Provoked DVT with a major transient risk factor (recent surgery) is treated for 3 months; longer therapy does not reduce recurrence risk meaningfully and increases bleeding. (Source: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE)
source_refs: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

explanation.summary: Duration of anticoagulation for VTE depends on whether the event was provoked, unprovoked or cancer-associated. Provoked VTE with a major transient risk factor (surgery, immobilisation, trauma, pregnancy) is treated for 3 months. Unprovoked VTE is treated for at least 3 to 6 months with consideration of extended therapy if bleeding risk is acceptable; rivaroxaban 10 mg or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily are validated extended secondary prevention options. Cancer-associated VTE is treated with a DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban) or LMWH for at least 6 months and continued while cancer is active. Routine thrombophilia screening does not change management in most cases.
key_points: Provoked VTE: 3 months of anticoagulation.
Unprovoked VTE: at least 3 to 6 months, consider extended.
Cancer-associated VTE: DOAC or LMWH while cancer active.
Routine thrombophilia screening rarely changes management.
Extended low-dose apixaban or rivaroxaban for select unprovoked cases.
explanation.pearls: The HERDOO2 rule identifies low-risk women with unprovoked VTE (under 50, no hyperpigmentation/oedema/redness, D-dimer under 250) who can safely stop anticoagulation at 3 months; men have no validated equivalent.

sources.label: ANZSBT and HSANZ Guidance on Anticoagulation in VTE

stem:
You are the ED registrar called to see a 62-year-old man with sudden onset severe ripping interscapular and chest pain that began 1 hour ago at rest. He has a history of poorly controlled hypertension. On examination there is a 28 mmHg difference between arms with the right side higher, an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge that was not previously documented, and reduced left radial pulse volume.

ECG shows sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy and no ST elevation. He is alert and his pain remains severe despite 5 mg of intravenous morphine.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 92, BP right arm 178/96, BP left arm 150/90, RR 22, SpO2 97%
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, early diastolic murmur LSE, reduced left radial volume, no focal neurology, chest clear, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus LVH no ST elevation, troponin 28 (mildly raised), chest x-ray widened mediastinum 9 cm, D-dimer 4.8, creatinine 92, lactate 1.6
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 4 mg daily; amlodipine 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence aspirin 300 mg and ticagrelor 180 mg, give a heparin bolus then infusion, and refer to cardiology for emergent percutaneous intervention for likely NSTEMI complicated by acute aortic regurgitation
options.rationale: Antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy is contraindicated in suspected aortic dissection; the clinical picture points to dissection, not NSTEMI. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Arrange transthoracic echocardiogram on the ward first to confirm the diagnosis before any CT imaging is performed, with cardiology consult once visualisation of the aortic root has been obtained
options.rationale: TTE has poor sensitivity for type B dissection and may delay definitive CT or TOE; contrast CT aortogram is the standard. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Administer thrombolysis with tenecteplase weight-adjusted bolus immediately based on the chest pain and elevated D-dimer, accepting that primary PCI is not available within the standard 120-minute window
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is catastrophic in aortic dissection causing fatal rupture or extension; ECG does not show STEMI. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Commence sodium nitroprusside infusion to rapidly lower blood pressure to target systolic 120, with intravenous morphine for analgesia and observation for spontaneous resolution of pain
options.rationale: Vasodilator alone causes reflex tachycardia and increased aortic wall stress; beta-blockade must precede or accompany vasodilator. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Commence intravenous esmolol infusion to target heart rate under 60 and systolic BP under 120, give intravenous opioid for analgesia, and arrange immediate contrast CT aortogram with surgical consult
options.rationale: Type A or B aortic dissection is treated with impulse control (heart rate then BP) and urgent CT aortogram with cardiothoracic surgical involvement. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular Aortic Dissection)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection presents with sudden severe ripping chest, back or interscapular pain; pulse deficits, blood pressure differential greater than 20 mmHg between arms, new aortic regurgitation, mediastinal widening and end-organ ischaemia are key supportive features. Initial management is impulse control - lower heart rate to under 60 with IV beta-blockade (esmolol or labetalol) THEN reduce systolic BP to 100 to 120 with vasodilator if still elevated; this minimises shear stress on the dissection flap. CT aortogram is the imaging gold standard; TOE is the bedside alternative. Stanford type A (ascending aorta) requires emergent cardiothoracic surgery; type B (descending only) is managed medically unless complicated.
key_points: Rate first then BP with IV beta-blockade.
CT aortogram is the diagnostic gold standard.
Type A = emergent surgery; Type B = medical unless complicated.
Antiplatelet, anticoagulant, thrombolysis contraindicated.
Pulse deficit and BP differential support diagnosis.
explanation.pearls: The pain-pulse-pressure triad of severe chest pain, BP differential greater than 20 mmHg, and pulse deficit identifies dissection earlier than imaging and should mandate empirical impulse control while CT is arranged.
